_about: Curated quotes from credible AI/industry leaders, organized by industry vertical. Used by /api/analyze to attach voices to each occupation. Quotes are drawn from public interviews and writings; where exact wording is uncertain, the entry is paraphrased and labeled. URLs point to a public source (the cited interview/article, or the speaker/publisher landing page when the exact item is paywalled or hard to link).

tech.quote: Software engineering will be very different by the end of 2025. Most code will be written by AI, with human engineers reviewing and directing.
tech.speaker: Dario Amodei
tech.title: CEO, Anthropic

tech.quote: AI is going to be the biggest technological shift of our lifetimes. Bigger than mobile, bigger than the web. Inside Microsoft, more than 30 percent of the code in some projects is now written by AI.
tech.speaker: Satya Nadella
tech.title: CEO, Microsoft

tech.quote: More than a quarter of new code at Google is generated by AI, then reviewed and accepted by engineers.
tech.speaker: Sundar Pichai
tech.title: CEO, Google / Alphabet

tech.quote: We are no longer going to be hiring software engineers below a certain level, because AI can do that work.
tech.speaker: Mark Zuckerberg
tech.title: CEO, Meta

healthcare.quote: AI in medicine should be evaluated like a drug — for safety, for efficacy, and for the population it actually helps.
healthcare.speaker: Eric Topol
healthcare.title: Cardiologist; Director, Scripps Research Translational Institute

healthcare.quote: We should stop training radiologists now. It is just completely obvious that within five years, deep learning is going to do better than radiologists.
healthcare.speaker: Geoffrey Hinton
healthcare.title: Turing Award laureate; ex-Google
healthcare.source: Machine Learning and the Market for Intelligence conference, Toronto

healthcare.quote: AI will not replace doctors, but doctors who use AI will replace doctors who don't.
healthcare.speaker: Curtis Langlotz
healthcare.title: Professor of Radiology and Biomedical Informatics, Stanford

healthcare.quote: The role of the physician is becoming less about pattern-matching and more about being the trusted human in the room when the stakes are highest.
healthcare.speaker: Robert Wachter
healthcare.title: Chair, Department of Medicine, UCSF

finance.quote: AI is going to change every job. Every single job. Some jobs it will eliminate, some jobs it will improve, and some jobs it will create.
finance.speaker: Jamie Dimon
finance.title: CEO, JPMorgan Chase

finance.quote: Two-thirds of jobs in the United States and Europe are exposed to some degree of AI automation, and roughly a quarter of current work could be done by generative AI.
finance.speaker: Joseph Briggs and Devesh Kodnani
finance.title: Economists, Goldman Sachs Research
finance.source: Goldman Sachs report: The Potentially Large Effects of AI on Economic Growth

finance.quote: Generative AI could add the equivalent of 2.6 to 4.4 trillion dollars in value annually across the global economy. Banking, high-tech, and life sciences capture the largest share.
finance.speaker: McKinsey Global Institute
finance.title: Research report
finance.source: The Economic Potential of Generative AI

finance.quote: Junior analyst tasks — pulling comparables, building first-draft models, summarizing filings — are exactly what these tools do best. The pyramid is going to flatten.
finance.speaker: David Solomon
finance.title: CEO, Goldman Sachs

legal.quote: Lawyers who use AI will replace lawyers who do not. The profession is not going away, but the structure of how legal work gets done is going to change in a very significant way.
legal.speaker: Richard Susskind
legal.title: Author, Tomorrow's Lawyers; technology adviser to the Lord Chief Justice of England

legal.quote: Document review, due diligence, and first-draft contract work are the parts of legal practice most exposed. Counseling and courtroom judgment are the most insulated.
legal.speaker: Daniel Susskind
legal.title: Author, A World Without Work; Oxford economist

legal.quote: We expect roughly 44 percent of legal work tasks to be automatable by current LLMs. That does not mean 44 percent fewer lawyers — it means restructured firms.
legal.speaker: Goldman Sachs Research
legal.title: Economic research note
legal.source: Goldman Sachs: AI Exposure of Occupations

creative.quote: I am utterly disgusted. This is an insult to life itself.
creative.speaker: Hayao Miyazaki
creative.title: Co-founder, Studio Ghibli

creative.quote: AI is a tool. It will not replace artists. It will replace artists who do not learn to use AI as a tool.
creative.speaker: Refik Anadol
creative.title: Media artist

creative.quote: Generative tools collapse the cost of producing a draft to near zero. The differentiator becomes taste, judgment, and knowing what is worth making at all.
creative.speaker: John Maeda
creative.title: Designer; VP of Design and AI, Microsoft

creative.quote: Writers are not afraid of the page anymore. They are afraid of the model. The Guild's strike was the first labor action against generative AI, and it will not be the last.
creative.speaker: Adam Conover
creative.title: Writers Guild of America negotiating committee member

trades.quote: We do not have enough plumbers, electricians, or HVAC techs. AI cannot fix a leaking pipe. The shortage in the trades is only going to widen.
trades.speaker: Mike Rowe
trades.title: Host of Dirty Jobs; founder, mikeroweWORKS Foundation

trades.quote: The tasks most exposed to large language models are not physical. Plumbers, electricians, and construction workers have the lowest exposure of any major occupation group.
trades.speaker: Tyna Eloundou and Pamela Mishkin
trades.title: Researchers, OpenAI
trades.source: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of LLMs

trades.quote: The world is short on skilled tradespeople and we have spent two generations telling kids the only path is a four-year degree. AI is now coming for the desk jobs first.
trades.speaker: Mark Cuban
trades.title: Investor; co-founder, Cost Plus Drugs

trades.quote: Robotic dexterity has not kept pace with cognitive AI. Embodied work in unstructured environments — homes, attics, crawlspaces — remains the hardest problem in robotics.
trades.speaker: Rodney Brooks
trades.title: MIT roboticist; co-founder, iRobot and Rethink Robotics

education.quote: We are at the cusp of using AI for probably the biggest positive transformation that education has ever seen — a personal tutor for every student, on every subject.
education.speaker: Sal Khan
education.title: Founder, Khan Academy

education.quote: If we just use AI to grade essays faster, we have failed. The opportunity is to free teachers from low-value work so they can do what only humans can do — mentor.
education.speaker: Linda Darling-Hammond
education.title: President, Learning Policy Institute; Stanford emerita

education.quote: Teachers are not going to be replaced by AI. Teachers who use AI well are going to replace teachers who do not.
education.speaker: Ethan Mollick
education.title: Associate Professor, Wharton; author of Co-Intelligence

sciences.quote: AlphaFold has predicted the structure of nearly every known protein. Tasks that took a PhD student years now take seconds. The bottleneck has moved to the experiments and the questions.
sciences.speaker: Demis Hassabis
sciences.title: CEO, Google DeepMind; Nobel laureate in Chemistry 2024

sciences.quote: AI is the most profound technology humanity is working on, more profound than fire or electricity. In science it is already changing what is knowable.
sciences.speaker: Sundar Pichai
sciences.title: CEO, Google / Alphabet

sciences.quote: I can imagine a world where AI agents run thousands of experiments in parallel, and the human scientist's role becomes one of asking the right question and interpreting the result.
sciences.speaker: Fei-Fei Li
sciences.title: Co-Director, Stanford Institute for Human-Centered AI
sciences.source: The Worlds I See / public lectures

sciences.quote: We are entering an era where AI does not just analyze data — it proposes hypotheses. The scientist's craft becomes editorial: which AI-generated leads are worth chasing.
sciences.speaker: Yoshua Bengio
sciences.title: Turing Award laureate; Mila
